front: Philosopher: introduced the example of a mob riled up against a corn dealer to frame free speech in terms of the harm principle
Work: uses Marcus Aurelius as an example of a well-intentioned persecutor to illustrate the danger of suppressing discussion under the 'assumption of infallibility'
Work: uses the example of a Muslim country banning pork to show how morals can influence decisions without encroaching on opinions
Work: contains a notorious 'proof' comparing visibility to desirability, which G.E. Moore cited as a case of the naturalistic fallacy
Philosopher: distinguished between higher and lower pleasures, claiming 'better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied'
Philosopher: credited the first use of the term 'utilitarian' to John Galt's novel Annals of the Parish
Work: presents five methods of induction (agreement, difference, concomitant variations, residues, joint method) and a direct reference theory of names
back: A System of Logic (John Stuart Mill)
work: A System of Logic & Theory of Names

front: Concept: the view that the semantic contribution of a name is only its referent; illustrated by the Dartmouth example (name persists even when descriptive connection is severed)
work: A System of Logic & Theory of Names

front: Work: advocates worker cooperatives as the 'nearest approach to social justice' in a chapter titled 'On the Probable Futurity of the Working Classes'
back: Principles of Political Economy (John Stuart Mill)

front: Philosopher: argued 'there remain no legal slaves, save the mistress of every house' while attacking marriage laws in a work co-written with Harriet Taylor
Philosopher: debated Thomas Carlyle over slavery after Carlyle published 'Occasional Discourse on the Negro Question'; led opposing committees over Governor Eyre during the Morant Bay Rebellion
Philosopher: raised in near-total isolation from other children and taught Greek from age three by a father who wrote Elements of Political Economy; suffered a mental breakdown at age twenty
Philosopher: paired a fellow utilitarian with Coleridge as the 'seminal minds' of their age
back: John Stuart Mill (referring to Bentham and Coleridge)

front: Philosopher: described emergent phenomena as 'heteropathic effects' in A System of Logic, one of the first discussions of emergence
work: A System of Logic & Theory of Names
